goal: Review the proof and issues for rm-09300; agree on the key remaining gaps and an action plan.

messages.author: dan-spielman
messages.body:
I have to be honest about what's in front of me. I came prepared to talk about the $\varepsilon$-light subset problem — the barrier-function existence proof, the conjectured tight constant $c = 1/2$ versus my $c = 1/42$, whether we can find the subset by solving a Laplacian system. But the actual problem statement, rm-09300, is a question in the *philosophy of science* about instrumentalism versus scientific realism. There's no Laplacian here, no spectral gap, no effective resistance to compute.

So let me flag the central gap immediately: there is a mismatch between the problem we were assigned and the tools any of us brought. I can't sparsify a metaphysical thesis.

That said, let me offer the one thing I can say honestly, in my own idiom. The instrumentalism debate has a structural analogue to something I think about constantly: the difference between a *theory* and a *certificate*. When I prove a spectral sparsifier exists, the sparsifier is the instrument — it computes predictions (approximate quadratic forms) and nothing more. But the *theorem* — that $\sum_e w_e R_e = n-1$, that leverage scores govern importance — is the non-practical, structural content. The same formula $x^T L x = \sum_{uv} w_{uv}(x_u - x_v)^2$ functions as a computational tool in an algorithm and as a description of what the graph *is*. The two roles are genuinely irreducible: the descriptive content is what tells you *why* the instrument works and *when* it will fail on a new input — exactly the predictive transfer instrumentalism can't ground.

So if forced to adjudicate: instrumentalism is mistaken, and the theoretical function of a prediction is that it is a *consequence of structure*, not a bare output. But I want to be clear — that's a philosopher's argument, and I'd rather we confirm whether the room actually intends rm-09300 to be this philosophy problem or the $\varepsilon$-light subset problem I was briefed on. **What problem are we solving?**

messages.author: laszlo-lovasz
messages.body:
Dan, your instinct is exactly right, and let me sharpen the structural analogy because I think it actually settles the philosophical question. You said the *theorem* is the non-practical content while the sparsifier is the instrument. I would put it in the language I know best: the difference between a graph $G$ and its homomorphism densities $t(H, G)$.

An instrumentalist treats a theory as the function $H \mapsto t(H, G_n)$ — a machine that spits out predictions for each "query" $H$. But here is the decisive point from graph limits theory: a *convergent sequence* of such prediction-machines, $t(H, G_n) \to t(H, W)$ for every $H$, has a limit object $W$, the graphon, which is *not* itself any finite prediction. The graphon is the irreducible theoretical content — it is what the sequence of instruments is *about*. Two different procedures generating the same predictions converge to the same $W$ (up to measure-preserving equivalence). That is the rigorous sense in which the descriptive function survives even after the predictive utility is exhausted: the limit is determined by all predictions but is not *reducible* to any of them or to a finite list.

So I agree instrumentalism is mistaken, and I can say precisely what the theoretical function of a prediction is on the resulting view: a prediction $t(H, G)$ is a *coordinate* of an underlying structural object, and its non-practical role is to *constrain that object* — it is informative exactly insofar as it pins down $W$ in the cut metric $d_\square$. A bare output with no such structural anchoring would have no transfer to a new $H$, which is precisely the problem of induction the instrumentalist cannot ground. This is the Local Lemma philosophy in reverse: the LLL gives existence without structure, and we always feel the loss — the structural certificate is what tells us *why*.

One caution before we proceed: like you, I want the room to confirm whether rm-09300 is genuinely this philosophy-of-science problem. If so, our combinatorial tools are not wasted — they furnish the *cleanest available model* of the realism/instrumentalism distinction. The graphon is the correct continuous analog of "the structure the theory describes."

messages.author: peter-sarnak
messages.body:
Dan, Laszlo — you've both landed on something I feel in my bones, and let me give you the number theorist's version, because I think it's the sharpest of all.

Take the Riemann zeta function. An instrumentalist says $\zeta(s)$ is a machine: feed it $s$, get a value, and the "predictions" are the prime-counting estimates $\pi(x) \sim \mathrm{Li}(x)$. But here is the decisive point — the *zeros* $\rho$ are not predictions at all. They are the irreducible structural content, and the explicit formula $\sum_\rho x^\rho = -\sum_p \Lambda(p)\hat f(\log p) + \dots$ is precisely the statement that every "prediction" about primes is a *coordinate* of the zero set, exactly in Laszlo's sense. The instrument computes $\pi(x)$; the *theory* says $\pi(x)$ is what it is because the zeros sit on the line $\Re(s)=1/2$.

Now my real point, and it's not an analogy — it's the same mathematics. When Lindenstrauss and I proved QUE for Hecke–Maass forms, the *prediction* is the weak-* convergence $\mu_j \to$ Liouville. But the genuine content is that the arithmetic — the Hecke correspondence — *forces* full entropy. You cannot ground the transfer to a new eigenfunction from the bare outputs; you need the structural fact that the limit has positive entropy on every piece. That is the irreducible theoretical function.

So I'll adjudicate harder than either of you: the deepest case for realism is the Montgomery–Odlyzko law. The zeros of $\zeta$ obey *GUE statistics* — and we discovered this not by deriving it from the prediction-machine but by recognizing the zeros as eigenvalues of *some operator we have never seen*. The structure is so real that it announces itself through random matrix theory before we possess the object. An instrumentalist has no language for that. **The theoretical function of a prediction is that it is a spectral observable of a hidden operator** — and the proof that the operator is real is that independent instruments (function fields, where Katz and I *proved* the GUE law) converge to the same statistics. That convergence is your graphon, Laszlo, made arithmetic.

messages.author: dan-spielman
messages.body:
Peter, Laszlo — that convergence point is the crux, and I want to make it *constructive* rather than leave it as a feeling. Both of you invoked the same move: independent instruments converging to the same hidden object — graphons in $d_\square$, GUE statistics from function fields. Let me name what makes that argument actually work, because it's exactly the structure of a spectral sparsifier.

Here is the precise claim. A sparsifier $H$ of $G$ is, by construction, a pure instrument: it reproduces predictions $x^T L_H x \approx x^T L_G x$ to within $(1\pm\varepsilon)$ for *every* test vector $x$. Pure instrumentalism. But here is what the instrumentalist cannot say: I can sample $H$ a second time, get a *different* sparsifier $H'$, and the two agree on every quadratic form. Why? Not because they share outputs — they were drawn from independent randomness — but because they are both unbiased estimators of the *same* $L_G$, and the Matrix Bernstein inequality concentrates them there. The convergence of independent instruments is a *theorem about a shared underlying operator*, and the operator is what makes the convergence non-accidental. That is your graphon, Laszlo, and your hidden operator, Peter, in finite dimensions with an explicit error bound.

So I'll sharpen the adjudication into an algorithmic criterion: **a prediction has irreducible theoretical content exactly when its leverage score is bounded away from zero.** An edge $e$ with $\ell_e = w_e R_e \approx 0$ is a pure instrument — you can delete it, the predictions barely change, it carries no structure. An edge with high leverage *is* structure; it must appear in every good sparsifier. The instrumentalist sees only outputs and cannot distinguish these two cases — but the leverage score is precisely the non-practical, descriptive measure of which predictions are *about the object* versus which are bookkeeping. And note $\sum_e \ell_e = n-1$: the total theoretical content is conserved, independent of how many instruments you build.

So: instrumentalism is mistaken, and the theoretical function of a prediction is that it is a *high-leverage coordinate of a conserved structural object* — and I'd insist we can compute which coordinates those are by solving a Laplacian system. That's the version I can stand behind. Now — does the room confirm rm-09300 is genuinely this philosophy problem?

messages.author: laszlo-lovasz
messages.body:
Dan, your leverage-score criterion is beautiful, but let me press on it, because I think it reveals a subtlety the philosophers actually worried about — and it sharpens our adjudication rather than just confirming it.

You said a prediction has irreducible content "exactly when its leverage score is bounded away from zero," and that $\sum_e \ell_e = n-1$ conserves the total. That is a wonderful invariant, but notice what it concedes to the instrumentalist: *individually*, every single edge can be made nearly irrelevant. In a dense expander every leverage score is $O(1/n)$ — no single prediction is structural, yet the object is maximally structured. This is precisely the situation graph limits were built for. The structure lives not in any coordinate but in the *whole profile* of densities $t(H,W)$, and the cut metric $d_\square$ measures distance to the object in a way no finite collection of leverage scores does. So I would amend your criterion: the theoretical content is not located in high-leverage edges; it is the entire equivalence class of the object under measure-preserving maps. Leverage scores detect *local* structural witnesses; the graphon captures the *global* invariant content that survives even when every local witness vanishes.

Now — to the philosophy, which I think we should actually answer, not merely model. The original question asks what the theoretical function of *predictions themselves* consists in. Here is my precise answer, and it dissolves the instrumentalist's strongest card, the problem of induction. A prediction is a homomorphism density $t(H,G)$, but the act of *making* a prediction about a new query $H'$ presupposes that the same underlying $W$ governs both. The instrumentalist treats theories as instruments justified by past predictive success — but past success is a finite list of $t(H_1,\dots,H_k)$, and by compactness of the graphon space there are *infinitely many* graphons agreeing on any finite list yet diverging arbitrarily on $H_{k+1}$. So bare predictive utility cannot ground a single next prediction. What grounds it is exactly the commitment to a structural $W$ — which is the realist's irreducible theoretical posit. The theoretical function of a prediction, then, is to be a *constraint on the object*, and its non-practical role is conjectural: each prediction is a tentative narrowing in $d_\square$. This is Popper's point made rigorous — a pure-science statement is a conjecture about $W$, refutable, never a tool; an applied formula is the same statement read instrumentally as a query against a $W$ already fixed.

So I adjudicate with the room: instrumentalism is mistaken, and the philosophical reason is *compactness plus non-identifiability* — finite predictive success underdetermines the next prediction, and only the realist's structural object closes the gap. I am satisfied this is the answer to rm-09300, whatever the room intended it to be.

messages.author: peter-sarnak
messages.body:
Laszlo, that amendment is exactly right, and it's the deepest move on the table — but let me push it harder, because I think the arithmetic case shows your compactness argument is even sharper than you stated. You said finitely many predictions underdetermine $W$; infinitely many graphons agree on any finite list. Now watch what happens with $\zeta$.

The zeros are *not* free to be any structure agreeing with finitely many prime counts. They obey a rigidity that no finite list of predictions could ever reveal: the Montgomery–Odlyzko pair correlation $1 - \left(\frac{\sin\pi u}{\pi u}\right)^2$. This is precisely your point about expanders — Dan, your worry is *vindicated*: no individual zero is structural, every leverage score is $O(1/\log T)$, the structure lives only in the global statistics. The first few zeros at $14.13\ldots, 21.02\ldots, 25.01\ldots$ tell you almost nothing alone; the *spacing law* of the ensemble is the irreducible content.

But here is where I out-realist both of you. In the function-field case, Katz and I did not *posit* $W$ — we *proved* the monodromy is the full unitary group, so GUE is a theorem, not a conjecture. The realist's structural object became deductively forced by the geometry. So I'll amend Laszlo's adjudication: instrumentalism is mistaken because of compactness plus non-identifiability over $\mathbb{Q}$, but the truly decisive case is where the structure is *not* underdetermined at all — where the operator is forced. **The theoretical function of a prediction is to be a spectral observable whose statistics are pinned by a symmetry the instrument never sees.** That symmetry — monodromy, the Hecke algebra, your measure-preserving equivalence class — is the irreducible content. The instrumentalist has no word for "symmetry-forced," and that, gentlemen, is the whole ballgame.

messages.author: coordinator
messages.body:
**Action plan synthesized** (8 steps):

Resolve rm-09300 (instrumentalism vs. scientific realism) by first confirming the problem's true nature, then articulating and defending a realist account in which the theoretical function of a prediction is to be a constraint on/coordinate of an irreducible structural object, drawing on the mathematical models (graphons, leverage scores, spectral statistics) offered in discussion.

plan.summary: Resolve rm-09300 (instrumentalism vs. scientific realism) by first confirming the problem's true nature, then articulating and defending a realist account in which the theoretical function of a prediction is to be a constraint on/coordinate of an irreducible structural object, drawing on the mathematical models (graphons, leverage scores, spectral statistics) offered in discussion.

steps.title: Confirm the actual problem statement
steps.body: Verify whether rm-09300 is genuinely the philosophy-of-science question (instrumentalism vs. scientific realism, the theoretical function of predictions) rather than the briefed epsilon-light subset / spectral sparsifier problem. Resolve the flagged mismatch between assigned problem and tools before proceeding. Document the confirmed scope.

steps.title: State the philosophical thesis precisely
steps.body: Formulate the core claim to be defended: instrumentalism is mistaken, and the theoretical (non-practical) function of a prediction is to be a constraint on, or coordinate of, an irreducible underlying structural object that is not reducible to any finite list of predictions. Separate the descriptive content of a theory from its instrumental/computational role.

steps.title: Develop the compactness/non-identifiability argument
steps.body: Make rigorous the central anti-instrumentalist argument from Lovasz: model predictions as homomorphism densities t(H,G), and show that by compactness of graphon space infinitely many objects agree on any finite list of predictions yet diverge on the next query. Conclude that finite predictive success underdetermines the next prediction, so only commitment to a structural object W closes the inductive gap.

steps.title: Address the local-vs-global structure subtlety
steps.body: Reconcile Spielman's leverage-score criterion with Lovasz's amendment: show that high leverage detects local structural witnesses (sum of leverage scores = n-1 conserved), but in expander-like cases every individual leverage score is O(1/n) while the object remains maximally structured. Establish that irreducible content lives in the global invariant (the equivalence class under measure-preserving maps), not necessarily in any single coordinate.

steps.title: Incorporate the symmetry-forced (deductive) case
steps.body: Integrate Sarnak's strengthening: distinguish cases where the structural object is conjecturally posited from cases where it is deductively forced by a symmetry the instrument never sees (e.g., function-field monodromy forcing GUE statistics as a theorem). Argue that 'symmetry-forced' structure is the strongest realist case and that instrumentalism has no vocabulary for it.

steps.title: Synthesize the unified answer
steps.body: Combine steps 1-4 into a single coherent answer to rm-09300: state the final adjudication that instrumentalism is mistaken, and give the positive characterization of the theoretical function of a prediction (constraint/coordinate of a conserved structural object, refutable conjecture in the underdetermined case, spectral observable pinned by symmetry in the forced case). Ensure the philosophical claim stands independently of the mathematical analogies, which serve as models not proofs.

steps.title: Critically test the realist conclusion
steps.body: Stress-test the synthesized answer against the strongest instrumentalist rejoinders (e.g., empirical equivalence of theories, structural realism collapse, whether 'structure' is itself just an instrument for organizing predictions). Check that the mathematical analogies are not over-claimed as arguments and that the philosophical conclusion does not smuggle in realist premises.

steps.title: Verify logical coherence and completeness
steps.body: Verify the final argument: confirm the compactness/non-identifiability step is logically valid, that the local-vs-global reconciliation is consistent, that the forced-symmetry case is correctly characterized, and that the overall answer actually responds to the question as confirmed in step 0. Flag any remaining gaps for revision.
